items.body:
### Summary
Mbed TLS unconditionally rejects any
X.509 CRL extension marked as critical and does not parse or process the content
of any extension, whether critical or not. This behavior is
noncompliant with RFC 5280, which requires implementations to support and correctly
handle certain standard extensions, especially when marked critical. By failing to
recognize or interpret even a single extension (https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/blob/development/library/x509_crl.c#L142), Mbed TLS rejects most CRLs encountered
in the wild that are otherwise valid and fully standards-compliant.


### System information

Mbed TLS version (number or commit id): 4.0.0 or older
Operating system and version: Ubuntu 24.04

### Expected behavior
Parse critical CRL extensions successfully


### Actual behavior
Rejects any CRL extension marked as critical; Thus, the whole CRL is rejected
items.title: Mbed TLS unconditionally rejects any X.509 CRL extension marked as critical

### Summary
For a given list of CRLs and a certificate, during certificate validation,  if the CRL corresponding to the certificate in question is missing–rendering its revocation status indeterminable–Mbed TLS does not report the result as undetermined. Instead, it silently accepts the certificate as valid.


### System information
Mbed TLS version (number or commit id): 4.0.0 or older
Operating system and version: Ubuntu 24.04

### Expected behavior
Reject the certificate as invalid since revocation status could not be determined from the given CRLs

### Actual behavior
Accepts the certificate without any warning / error messages to the user regarding undetermined revocation status

items.body:
A PK context currently has the following fields:
- `pk_info` (legacy, on its way out but we're not there yet)
- `psa_type`
- `priv_id`
- `pub_raw`, `pub_raw_len`
- `bits`
- `ec_family` (if EC enabled)

The theory/goal is that once a context is populated, by using one of the 5 public APIs that populate contexts (wrap PSA, copy (public) from PSA, parse (public)), then all those fields (except currently `priv_id` for public keys) have non-zero values that are consistent which each other.

This is not directly tested so far. It's only indirectly tested by ensuring that the functions that operate on a PK context work with each of the 5 possible ways of populating the context. However that's not a good strategy: for example parsing has several code paths, and it was [recently discovered](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/580#discussion_r2720331126) that one of them was not populating `pub_raw` as it should. It would be impractical to require that each function that operates on a PK context be tested not just with all of the 5 methods of populating, but also exercise all the cases for each method (parsing PEM vs DER etc).

Instead we want to have a central test helper function that validates all the invariants that a populated PK context should satisfy, and then call that helper in each of the existing test functions for parsing (and other methods of populating a context - won't repeat that part). Assuming we already had test data exercising each code path of parsing, this will ensure that parsing always correctly populates all the fields of the context. Then functions that operate on a PK context can just rely on it and never get bad surprises.

Note: `ec_family` is actually redundant and can be extracted from `psa_type`. Removing that redundancy is out of scope for this task - edit: see #10583
items.title: PK: properly test invariants after populating a context

The `generate_driver_wrappers.py` script supports several parameters:

```less
usage: generate_driver_wrappers.py [-h] [--project-root PROJECT_ROOT] [--template-dir TEMPLATE_DIR] [--json-dir JSON_DIR] [output_directory]

positional arguments:
  output_directory      output file's location

options:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --project-root PROJECT_ROOT
                        root directory of the repo source code
  --template-dir TEMPLATE_DIR
                        directory holding the driver templates
  --json-dir JSON_DIR
                        directory holding the driver JSONs
```

However, at the moment it is not possible to pass these parameters to the script via CMake. Specifically, it is not possible to invoke
`cmake --template-dir path/to/template/dir --json-dir /path/to/driverlist.json/folder`,
so I would like to ask whether support for this functionality could be added.

We are currently working on developing a PSA driver for the STSAFE-A110 secure element, and I noticed that the `psa_crypto_driver_wrappers.h.jinja` template does not yet support all entry points, in particular `sign_hash` and `verify_hash` (and several others). This requires us to patch the `psa_crypto_driver_wrappers.h.jinja` template, but we would like to avoid modifying the original template. Instead, we would like to copy it into our own sources and then patch it. This way, when we update the MbedTLS library to a newer version, we will not need to worry about patching the template again. To do this, however, it is necessary to pass the template directory to `generate_driver_wrappers.py`, and we would like to avoid calling this script directly, preferring instead to leverage the existing MbedTLS build mechanism which already calls `generate_driver_wrappers.py`.

### Summary

`mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_file` fails when the public key in a certificate is an RSASSA-PSS public key (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.1.10). The error is `-0x3c80` (`MBEDTLS_ERR_PK_UNKNOWN_PK_ALG`).

### System information

Mbed TLS version (number or commit id): 3.6.5
Operating system and version: Ubuntu 24.04
Configuration (if not default, please attach `mbedtls_config.h`): default configuration
Compiler and options (if you used a pre-built binary, please indicate how you obtained it): gcc, RSASSA-PSS is enabled (`#define MBEDTLS_X509_RSASSA_PSS_SUPPORT`)

### Expected behavior

`mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_file` succeeds to parse certificates containing an RSASSA-PSS key.

### Actual behavior


`mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_file` fails with `-0x3c80` (`MBEDTLS_ERR_PK_UNKNOWN_PK_ALG`).

### Steps to reproduce

The simplest way I've found to generate a certificate containing an RSASSA-PSS key is to use a TPM2, and the openssl-tpm2 provider:

```sh
openssl genpkey -provider tpm2 -provider default -algorithm RSA-PSS -pkeyopt bits:2048 -pkeyopt digest:sha256 -out /tmp/tpm-rsapss.priv
openssl req -provider tpm2 -provider default -key /tmp/tpm-rsapss.priv -new -sha256 -subj '/CN=rsapss key' -out /tmp/tpm-rsapss.cert.pem -x509
```

Alternatively, the following certificate can be used:


The issue is demonstrated by the `cert_app` example program (`programs/x509/cert_app`):

```console
$ cert_app mode=file filename=/tmp/tpm-rsapss.cert.pem
  . Loading the CA root certificate ... ok (1 skipped)

  . Loading the certificate(s) ... failed
  !  mbedtls_x509_crt_parse_file returned -15488

```

### Additional information

To fix this, multiple changes seems to be necessary (but I'm not sure they're sufficient to manage all cases):

- the rsassaPss key type must be added to `oid_pk_alg[]`, e.g. with

    ```c
    {
        OID_DESCRIPTOR(MBEDTLS_OID_RSASSA_PSS,          "rsassaPss",        "RSASSA-PSS"),
        MBEDTLS_PK_RSASSA_PSS,
    },
    ```

- in `pk_get_pk_alg`, parameters should be parsed for RSASSA-PSS keys (or maybe simply ignored, since apart from the padding, they are not defined in `mbedtls_rsa_context`?)
- in `mbedtls_pk_info_from_type`, the `MBEDTLS_PK_RSASSA_PSS` type should also be handled (possibly the same way as for `MBEDTLS_PK_RSA`)
- in `mbedtls_pk_parse_subpubkey`, the `MBEDTLS_PK_RSASSA_PSS` type should be handled (possibly the same way as `MBEDTLS_PK_RSA`, to the padding exception)

I've encountered this issue on v3.6.5, but it seems to also be applicable on the [builtin driver of TF-PSA-Crypto v1.0.0](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/blob/76920edddcad00ac41b248e12d937b845df7bedb/drivers/builtin/src/oid.c#L123-L126)

There is a similar issue in [`x509write_csr_der_internal`](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/blob/e185d7fd85499c8ce5ca2a54f5cf8fe7dbe3f8df/library/x509write_csr.c#L239-L249): the signature OID is systematically the PKCS#1 v1.5 one.
This is probably also the case in `mbedtls_x509write_crt_der` (and basically everywhere `mbedtls_asn1_write_algorithm_identifier_ext` is called)

items.body:
Populated PK contexts are now much more uniform than they used to be, see https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/10573

We can take advantage of this to simplify the code in a number of places. This issue is not mean to be comprehensive, but to reap easily-discovered low-hanging fruit. Below is what I've noticed so far.

Since `pub_raw` is always populated, we should never have to call `psa_export_public_key()`, except for the purpose of populating `pub_raw` in the first place). Taking a quick look, I think all the calls to `psa_export_public_key()` in `pkwrite.c` and `pk_wrap.c` can be removed, as well as the call inside `import_public_into_psa()` in `pk.c` (the other two calls in `pk.c` seem valid though).
 
Since all private keys now use `priv_id`, not just "opaque" keys (now called "wrapping" key), a lot of places where we used to distinguish between `PK_OPAQUE` and other types can now be easily unified. Basically, the main difference remaining between wrapping (`PK_OPAQUE`) keys and non-wrapping keys is that for wrapping keys we don't own the PSA key and must not destroy it. (The other difference is wrapping keys might have more varied policies, but hopefully we shouldn't have to worry in PK and leave policy question to PSA.) So, a lot of places in the code with `if PK_OPAQUE` or `case PK_OPAQUE` can probably be simplified. Again, this issue is not about simplifying everything, just the low-hanging fruit. (I mean, [in one case](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/blob/d3b3411a26c26db1a998b10b072712e945518e63/drivers/builtin/src/pkwrite.c#L147) we have an if-else with identical code in both branches...)

We could also remove `ec_family` from `pk_context` as it is now fully redundant.

Any other easy simplification discovered in the process is fair game for this task (as long as the resulting PR isn't too large). (Btw, we may want to make multiple PRs for this issue, one for each kind of think we're simplifying, depending on their size.)

**Depends on:** #10573 (in case the improved testing uncovers bugs that will then be fixed in the same PR - said otherwise, let's fully test the invariants before we fully take advantage of them).
items.title: PK: simplify code by relying on the new invariants

I apologize if I am not posting this at the right place. It is about both Mbed TLS, PSA Crypto API, and PSA Crypto Driver Interface. I chose here because, even if there are implications for PSA, it is first about Mbed TLS. If it's better to have it elsewhere, let me know and I'll move it.

My use-case is about using a key stored in a secure element for performing multiple cryptographic operations, including TLS session establishment.

Today, on branch 3.x, I'm relying on custom `pk_ctx` and `mbedtls_pk_info_t` to implement all cryptographic operations related to a secure element. I also have a custom key format and some modifications to `mbedtls_pk_parse_key` so that appropriate key contexts are created when parsing a key.

With the PSA Crypto API, I was (maybe mistakenly) thinking that Mbed TLS would rely on PSA key ids (`psa_key_id_t`) for TLS session establishment, and that supporting a secure element would consist in implementing the [PSA Crypto Driver Interface](https://arm-software.github.io/psa-api/crypto-driver/1.0/111106-PSA_Certified_Crypto_Driver_Interface-1.0-alp.1.pdf) for that secure element as an opaque driver (with some current limitations: e.g. [the lack of driver-specific key attributes](https://github.com/ARM-software/psa-api/issues/326)).

However, I'm seeing that the functions in `ssl.h` do not use `psa_key_id_t` at all, instead they rely on `mbedtls_pk_context`. This is e.g. the case for `mbedtls_ssl_conf_own_cert`, whose signature is unchanged from 3.6.5:

```c
int mbedtls_ssl_conf_own_cert(mbedtls_ssl_config *conf,
                              mbedtls_x509_crt *own_cert,
                              mbedtls_pk_context *pk_key);
```

What would be the best way to support this use-case in version 4.x? Is it as previously, by using a custom `mbedtls_pk_info_t` and `pk_ctx` (with all functions, e.g. `sign_func()`, redirecting to the PSA Crypto Driver)? Or is there another way I've missed?

I'm also seeing in https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/10573 that `mbedtls_pk_info_t` might be deprecated in future versions, but I've not found any information on what would be the replacement.

### Suggested enhancement

Provide guidance on how to implement and use secure elements from Mbed TLS, either via PSA opaque driver, or another mechanism.

items.body:
Since PolarSSL, our config header contains
```
#if defined(_MSC_VER) && !defined(_CRT_SECURE_NO_DEPRECATE)
#define _CRT_SECURE_NO_DEPRECATE 1
#endif
```

We do this because when we build the library, we want to be able to use some functions that MSVC doesn't like, such as `fopen()`. But we shouldn't impose this on third-party applications. Depending on whether you include system headers before or after an Mbed TLS or TF-PSA-Crypto header, you will or will not get a warning if you use a function that MSVC considers deprecated.

We should only define `_CRT_SECURE_NO_DEPRECATE` when building our own code, i.e. in `common.h` in 3.6 and in `*_platform_requirements.h` in [1.1](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/TF-PSA-Crypto/pull/694)/[4.1](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/pull/10606).

items.body:
### Suggested enhancement

The macros `MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V15` and `MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V21` are internal crypto macros, which we should no longer be using on the Mbed TLS side.

Originally, each of these macros might mean either encryption or signature. Fortunately, X.509 uses only signatures, and these days so does TLS (we've removed the TLS 1.2 key exchanges based on RSA encryption in Mbed TLS 4.0: [RSA-PSK](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/9681) and [RSA](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/9682)).

So, this should be as simple as:
- `s/MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V15/PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN/`
- `s/MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V21/PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PSS/`

Well, except that PSA has variants for these algs:
- `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN` vs `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN_RAW`
- `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PSS` vs `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PSS_ANY_SALT`

So perhaps we first need to have some abstraction macros such as `PSA_HAVE_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN_ANY` and `PSA_HAVE_RSA_PSS_ANY`? A bit like `PSA_HAVE_ALG_ECDSA_SIGN` which means we can create either randomized or deterministic ECDSA signatures. (If we add those, should they live on the crypto side or the mbedtls side?)

And then it's a simple substitution from the legacy macros to these new light abstraction macros.

### Justification

Mbed TLS needs this because using crypto-internal feature macros is a thing of the past.
items.title: Quit using `MBEDTLS_PKCS1_Vxx` macros

items.body:
### Suggested enhancement

The macro `MBEDTLS_RSA_C` is an internal crypto macro which we should no longer be using on the Mbed TLS side.

Unfortunately, its meaning does not match a PSA macro exactly: in some cases the replacement should be `PSA_WANT_KEY_TYPE_RSA_PUBLIC_KEY`, and in some cases it should be `PSA_WANT_KEY_TYPE_RSA_KEYPAIR_xxx`.

However since we currently don't have good support for builds supporting only public keys of a given key type, it is probably acceptable to just use `PSA_WANT_KEY_TYPE_RSA_KEYPAIR_BASIC` everywhere.

### Justification

Mbed TLS needs this because using crypto-internal feature macros is a thing of the past.

items.body:
### Suggested enhancement

This macro, as its documentation says, is about parsing and verifying certificates that are signed using RSA-PSS by the issuing CA.

This has absolutely nothing to do with whether the public key contained in the certificate (or the associated private key) is going to be used to verify (or create) RSA-PSS signatures. In fact, as certificate could be signed with RSA-PSS by its issuing CA, while containing an ECC key. Or it would be signed with ECDSA by its issuing CA and contain an RSA key, that could be used to verify PSS signatures.

Therefore, using this macro in TLS code (mostly 1.3) to decide whether an RSA-PSS _handshake_ signature can be done was always wrong.

We should fix it, and fix the dependencies in the tests as well.

### Justification

Mbed TLS needs this because our dependencies are already complex enough without misusing our own macros.

```
{
```

items.body:
In the PKCS12 module, the code to determine the block size for the PKCS#12 KDF follows [RFC 7292](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7292#appendix-B.2). SHA3 didn't exist when RFC 7292 was written. The code is a bit sloppy, and it decides that the block size for a SHA3 variant is the same as for the SHA2 variant with the same size. But in practice, for PKCS12 with SHA3, people use the same block size as for HMAC, which is different.

The PKCS12 module should either reject SHA3 or use the unofficial, but widely accepted block size.

Reported privately by Kr0emer. We are re-filing this publicly because we do not see a security issue, merely functional non-compliance.

PKCS12 is rather obsolescent (it uses DES!), so it's unlikely that it's actually used with SHA3 in practice. Therefore we consider this a low-importance bug.

Our PKCS12 module [also used the wrong block size for MD2](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/4267).

labels.description: The issue is resolved in 4.1 onwards, but still present in 3.6 LTS.
labels.name: resolved-after-3.6

items.body:
In our project, we define a custom TF-PSA-Crypto configuration file (`my_crypto_config.h`) that overrides the memory allocation functions using `my_calloc()` and `my_free()` defined in a custom header `my_memory_header.h`.

`my_crypto_config.h`:
```c
#include "my_memory_header.h" // Defines `my_calloc()` and `my_free()`.

/* Platform porting support */
#define MBEDTLS_PLATFORM_C
#define MBEDTLS_PLATFORM_MEMORY
#define MBEDTLS_PLATFORM_CALLOC_MACRO my_calloc
#define MBEDTLS_PLATFORM_FREE_MACRO   my_free
```

To support this, we create an `INTERFACE` target `memory_target` that adds the include directory for `my_memory_header.h`:
```cmake
add_library(memory_target INTERFACE)
target_include_directories(memory_target INTERFACE "dir/with/memory_header") # Directory with `my_memory_header.h`.

# Set the option with the user TF-PSA-Crypto configuration file.
set(TF_PSA_CRYPTO_CONFIG_FILE "path/to/my_crypto_config.h" CACHE STRING "" FORCE)

# Add MbedTLS as a subdirectory.
add_subdirectory("path/to/mbedtls")
```

We link `memory_target` to the public Mbed TLS targets (`mbedtls`, `mbedx509`, `tfpsacrypto`) without issues.
```cmake
# Link these 3 public Mbed TLS targets with `memory_target`.
target_link_libraries(mbedtls PRIVATE memory_target)
target_link_libraries(mbedx509 PRIVATE memory_target)
target_link_libraries(tfpsacrypto PRIVATE memory_target)
```

However, some internal targets (`p256-m`, `builtin`, `everest`, `pqcp`) also include `my_crypto_config.h` but do not receive the include directories from `memory_target` via Mbed TLS's CMake configuration.
Ideally users should not link against these internal targets, and indeed they are **not advertised in the Mbed TLS documentation as targets intended for public use**. But because Mbed TLS's CMake does not propagate `memory_target` to the following internal targets during compilation, the build fails due to missing include directory for `my_memory_header.h`.
Currently, the only solution is to link `memory_target` directly to these internal targets:
```cmake
# Link Mbed TLS internal targets with `memory_target` because includes are missing
target_link_libraries(p256-m PRIVATE memory_target)
target_link_libraries(builtin PRIVATE memory_target)
target_link_libraries(everest PRIVATE memory_target)
target_link_libraries(pqcp PRIVATE memory_target)
```

Without these explicit links, these internal targets are **not aware of the include directory needed for `my_crypto_config.h`**, and the build fails.

### Expected behavior

Mbed TLS’s CMake build should propagate include requirements (e.g., from `memory_target`) to all targets that compile user configuration headers such as `my_crypto_config.h`, including internal targets (`p256‑m`, `builtin`, `everest`, `pqcp`).

### Actual behavior

The include directory from `memory_target` is propagated only to the public targets (`mbedtls`, `mbedx509`, `tfpsacrypto`), but not to the internal ones, causing missing headers and build failures.

### Proposal

Either:

* Propagate `memory_target` to all internal targets that compile user config headers;
* Define a CMake option through which the user can provide a list of directories to be included in all targets (public and internal) compiled by Mbed TLS;
* Document which internal targets must be explicitly linked when using custom config headers that depend on external includes.
items.title: [cmake] Propagate custom include directories to all Mbed TLS targets (public and internal)

### Suggested enhancement
In OpenSSL it is possible to call `BIO_get_conn_address` to get the address of the connected peer for a socket. This is not currently possible in mbedtls as far as I can tell. Perhaps an API among the lines of `mbedtls_net_get_remote_addr` could be nice here.

### Justification
`mbedtls_net_connect` calls `getaddrinfo` to resolve hostnames to IP addresses. If I want to know the IP address that the socket is effectively connected/connecting to, that is currently not possible unless I call `getaddrinfo` outside of `mbedtls_net_connect`. (I'm unsure if `getaddrinfo` is guaranteed to return the same result or cache the result if they are called twice like this.)

### Suggested enhancement
Make `mbedtls_ssl_(session|context)_load()` functions memory-safe even when provided with malformed or unexpected input, for example data not produced by `mbedtls_ssl_(session|context)_save()`.
                                                                                
This would act as a second line of defense as serialized TLS data is expected to
be protected against tampering by the application. See https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls-docs/blob/main/security-advisories/mbedtls-security-advisory-2026-03-serialized-data.md for additional information.

### Justification
Although applications are responsible for ensuring the integrity of serialized TLS data, making deserialization functions resilient to malformed input reduces the risk of undefined behavior (such as out-of-bounds accesses) in case this assumption is violated. This improves the overall robustness and defense in depth of the library.

items.body:
## Summary

The implementation does not fully enforce the TLS 1.3 requirement that, when the server is able to provide a certificate chain whose certificates are signed with algorithms advertised by the client, it should select and send such a compliant chain.  
Static analysis shows that the certificate selection logic only matches the client’s advertised **CertificateVerify** signature schemes and key type compatibility, but does not evaluate the signature hash algorithms used across the configured certificate chain. Runtime testing further confirms this gap: even when both a SHA-1-signed chain and a SHA-256-signed chain are simultaneously available, the server may still select the SHA-1-signed chain despite the client advertising only SHA-2 signature schemes.  
This creates a reproducible standards-conformance issue with practical interoperability, observability, and weak-chain exposure implications.

## 1. Requirement (RFC 8446 / TLS1.3.txt)

Specification file: `D:\project\conditionFuzzing\document\TLS1.3.txt`

Relevant original text:

```text
3726:    All certificates provided by the server MUST be signed by a signature
3727:    algorithm advertised by the client if it is able to provide such a
3728:    chain (see Section 4.2.3).  Certificates that are self-signed or
3729:    certificates that are expected to be trust anchors are not validated
3730:    as part of the chain and therefore MAY be signed with any algorithm.
3731:
3732:    If the server cannot produce a certificate chain that is signed only
3733:    via the indicated supported algorithms, then it SHOULD continue the
3734:    handshake by sending the client a certificate chain of its choice
3735:    that may include algorithms that are not known to be supported by the
3736:    client.  This fallback chain SHOULD NOT use the deprecated SHA-1 hash
3737:    algorithm in general, but MAY do so if the client's advertisement
3738:    permits it, and MUST NOT do so otherwise.
```

Core requirement:

- If the server **is able** to provide a compliant chain, all sent certificates should match the client-advertised algorithms.

## 2. Code Path Analysis

### 2.1 Certificate Selection Logic (Server)

File: `mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls13_server.c`

- Function: `ssl_tls13_pick_key_cert()`
- Key lines:
  - `1123-1129`: iterates over client signature algorithms (`received_sig_algs`) and checks whether they are offered / usable for `CertificateVerify`.
  - `1132-1177`: iterates over configured key-cert pairs and selects the first pair whose key matches the chosen signature scheme.
  - `1160-1165`: checks only key/signature scheme compatibility (`mbedtls_ssl_tls13_check_sig_alg_cert_key_match`, `mbedtls_pk_can_do_psa`).

Critical observation:

- The selection path does **not** evaluate the signature hash algorithm of each certificate in the chain (for example, SHA-1 vs SHA-256 in certificate signatures).

### 2.2 Certificate Message Writing Path

File: `mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls13_generic.c`

- Function: `ssl_tls13_write_certificate_body()`
- Key lines:
  - `775-791`: writes full chain entries (`while (crt != NULL)`) directly from the configured certificate chain.

Critical observation:

- The writing path serializes whichever chain was selected; it does not apply additional filtering based on certificate-signature algorithms.

### 2.3 Multiple-Certificate Ordering Behavior

File: `mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls.c`

- `mbedtls_ssl_conf_own_cert()` -> `ssl_append_key_cert()`
- Key lines:
  - `1627-1660`: append behavior preserves insertion order.

Implication:

- When multiple certificates are configured and all pass key/signature-scheme checks, earlier entries can be selected even if their certificate-signature algorithms are weaker.

## 3. Runtime Validation

### 3.1 Existing Weak-Hash Runtime Evidence (Single Certificate)

Observed:

- The client advertised RSA SHA-2 schemes (for example, `rsa_pkcs1_sha256`) and no SHA-1 scheme.
- The server still used a certificate signed with SHA-1.
- The handshake failed during client certificate verification.

This confirms a behavioral inconsistency in practical deployment, but a single-certificate setup alone cannot prove the **"if able"** branch.

### 3.2 Dual-Certificate Runtime Test (Proves the "if able" Condition)

Test design:

- One CA signs two server leaf certificates:
  - Certificate `#1`: `sha1WithRSAEncryption`
  - Certificate `#2`: `sha256WithRSAEncryption`
- Both certificates are loaded simultaneously into `ssl_server2` (`crt_file` and `crt_file2`).
- The client advertises only SHA-2 signature schemes (default TLS 1.3 set as shown in mbedTLS test client logs).

Key results (`result.txt`):

```text
client_rc=1
sha1_cert_sig=... Signature Algorithm: sha1WithRSAEncryption
sha256_cert_sig=... Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
...
593: ... selected signature algorithm rsa_pss_rsae_sha512 [0806]
594: ... selected certificate (chain) #1:
601: ... signed using      : RSA with SHA1
...
683: ... mbedtls_ssl_handshake returned -0x2700
691: ... Certificate verification failed
```

Interpretation:

- The server had an available SHA-256 certificate chain but still selected the SHA-1 chain.
- Therefore, even in a scenario where a compliant choice exists, the implementation does not enforce the requirement.

## 4. Inconsistency Conclusion

Conclusion:

- This is a real and reproducible inconsistency.
- The implementation matches the client-offered **CertificateVerify** algorithms and key type, but does not enforce certificate-signature algorithm constraints across the sent certificate chain.
- Under dual-certificate availability, observed behavior confirms non-compliant selection with respect to the **"if able"** requirement.

## 5. Security Impact Assessment

Risk type:

- Policy-conformance security risk with practical interoperability and observability impact.

Potential impacts:

### 5.1 Increased Handshake Failure Rate (Availability Impact)

Clients with strict verification profiles reject weak-hash chains, causing avoidable handshake failures even when a compliant chain is available.

### 5.2 Behavior Fingerprinting Surface

Distinct failures caused by weak-signed chain selection can reveal aspects of the server-side certificate-selection strategy.

### 5.3 Weak-Chain Usage Exposure in Permissive Clients

If client policy is lax, weak-signed chains may be accepted, weakening the overall security posture.

Current practical severity:

- Medium.

## 6. Boundary Conditions and Notes

- The RFC explicitly allows exceptions for self-signed certificates and trust anchors.
- This analysis avoids that ambiguity by using a controlled dual-certificate setup and proving that a stronger alternative was actually available.
- Runtime evidence and static path analysis are consistent.

## 7. Mitigation Direction (Without Modifying mbedTLS Source)

If the source code must remain unchanged:

1. Do not configure SHA-1/MD5-signed server certificates or chains in deployment.
2. Keep only SHA-256+ chains in `crt_file` / `crt_file2` candidates.
3. Treat certificate inventory hygiene as a mandatory CI/CD gate.

Note:

- Existing profile controls (for example, verification-side certificate profile settings) mainly affect peer certificate validation and do not fully solve the server-side chain selection semantics discussed here.

items.body:
### Suggested enhancement
RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3 specification), Section 5.1, states:

Handshake messages MAY be coalesced into a single TLSPlaintext record
or fragmented across several records, provided that:
- ...
-Handshake messages MUST NOT span key changes. Implementations
MUST verify that all messages immediately preceding a key change
align with a record boundary; if not, then they MUST terminate the
connection with an "unexpected_message" alert. Because the
ClientHello, EndOfEarlyData, ServerHello, Finished, and KeyUpdate
messages can immediately precede a key change, implementations
MUST send these messages in alignment with a record boundary.

Currently, we do not "verify that all messages immediately preceding a key change
align with a record boundary".

This issue is to investigate how to verify that all messages immediately preceding a key change
align with a record boundary.

### Justification

Mbed TLS needs this to comply with RFC 8446.
items.title: TLS 1.3: Enforce that handshake messages do not span key changes

### Summary
I'm using mbed TLS in the Hiawatha webserver. It has a reverse proxy functionality, which used to work well with mbed TLS v3 but not with v4. When forwarding a HTTP request to another webserver, I sometimes get a MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_RECEIVED_NEW_SESSION_TICKET from mbedtls_ssl_read(). It's not listed as a possible return from that function and online documentation says it's experimental. Sounds to me like this should not happen.


### System information

Operating system and version: Ubuntu server 24.04.4 LTS

### Expected behavior
Not to get an undocumented return from mbedtls_ssl_read().


### Actual behavior
mbedtls_ssl_read() sometimes returns MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_RECEIVED_NEW_SESSION_TICKET. When I treat it the same way as MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_WANT_READ, it (mostly) works. But that feels hackish.


### Steps to reproduce
Install the latest version of the Hiawatha webserver and configure a reverse proxy:
ReverseProxy = .* https://some.website/

# mbedTLS TLS 1.3 weak-hash certificate alert mapping inconsistency

## 1. Problem Summary

- SHA-1 case: mbedTLS rejects certificates that require SHA-1 for validation, but the alert sent is not consistently `bad_certificate(42)`.
- MD5 case: mbedTLS rejects certificates that require MD5 for validation, but the alert sent is not consistently `bad_certificate(42)`.

In both cases the handshake fails (correct), but the alert code observed at runtime is `certificate_unknown(46)` rather than `bad_certificate(42)`.

## 2. RFC 8446 Source Text

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2.2):

```text
Any endpoint receiving any certificate which it would need to
validate using any signature algorithm using an MD5 hash MUST abort
the handshake with a "bad_certificate" alert.  SHA-1 is deprecated,
and it is RECOMMENDED that any endpoint receiving any certificate
which it would need to validate using any signature algorithm using a
SHA-1 hash abort the handshake with a "bad_certificate" alert.
```

Alert code definitions from [RFC 8446, Section 6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6.2):

- `bad_certificate(42)`: a certificate was corrupt, contained signatures that did not verify correctly, etc.
- `certificate_unknown(46)`: some other (unspecified) issue arose in processing the certificate.

## 3. Code Description (mbedTLS)

### 3.1 Weak-hash certificates are rejected at the X.509 layer

The default verification profile only allows SHA-256/384/512, from `library/x509_crt.c`:

```c
const mbedtls_x509_crt_profile mbedtls_x509_crt_profile_default =
{
    MBEDTLS_X509_ID_FLAG(MBEDTLS_MD_SHA256) |
    MBEDTLS_X509_ID_FLAG(MBEDTLS_MD_SHA384) |
    MBEDTLS_X509_ID_FLAG(MBEDTLS_MD_SHA512),
    ...
};
```

The SSL default configuration uses this profile, from `library/ssl_tls.c`:

```c
conf->cert_profile = &mbedtls_x509_crt_profile_default;
```

When a certificate's signing hash is not accepted by the profile, `MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_BAD_MD` is set, from `library/x509_crt.c`:

```c
if (x509_profile_check_md_alg(profile, child->sig_md) != 0) {
    *flags |= MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_BAD_MD;
}
```

### 3.2 Alert mapping does not have a dedicated branch for `BAD_MD`

The alert selection logic in `library/ssl_tls.c`:

```c
if (ssl->session_negotiate->verify_result & MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_OTHER) {
    alert = MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_ACCESS_DENIED;
} else if (ssl->session_negotiate->verify_result & MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_CN_MISMATCH) {
    alert = MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_BAD_CERT;
} ...
else if (ssl->session_negotiate->verify_result & MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_NOT_TRUSTED) {
    alert = MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_UNKNOWN_CA;
} else {
    alert = MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_CERT_UNKNOWN;
}
```

There is no branch for `MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_BAD_MD`, so weak-hash failures fall through to the default `CERT_UNKNOWN(46)`.

Alert constant values from `include/mbedtls/ssl.h`:
- `MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_BAD_CERT = 42`
- `MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_CERT_UNKNOWN = 46`

## 4. Runtime Evidence

SHA-1 case:
- `client_rc=1` (handshake failed, certificate rejected)
- `send alert level=2 message=46`

MD5 case:
- `client_rc=1` (handshake failed, certificate rejected)
- `send alert level=2 message=46`

Neither case produced `message=42`.

## 5. Inconsistency Analysis

### MD5 case — stronger inconsistency

- RFC requirement: `MUST ... bad_certificate` (mandatory).
- Implementation: certificate is rejected, but alert may be `certificate_unknown(46)`, not `bad_certificate(42)`.
- Classification: rejection behavior satisfied, alert semantics not satisfied.

### SHA-1 case — weaker inconsistency

- RFC requirement: `RECOMMENDED ... bad_certificate` (recommended).
- Implementation: certificate is rejected, but alert is not mapped to `bad_certificate` as recommended.
- Classification: primary security action satisfied, recommended alert semantics not aligned.

### Root cause

- The X.509 layer classifies weak-hash failures as `BAD_MD`.
- The SSL alert mapping table has no dedicated branch for `BAD_MD` → `BAD_CERT(42)`.
- Weak-hash certificate failures therefore fall through to the default `CERT_UNKNOWN(46)`.

## 6. Conclusion

Current mbedTLS behavior for both cases:

- SHA-1 and MD5 certificates are rejected (correct).
- But the alert is not consistently mapped to `bad_certificate(42)` as required/recommended by RFC 8446.
items.title: mbedTLS TLS 1.3 weak-hash certificate alert mapping inconsistency

# mbedTLS TLS 1.3 X.509 `cert_data` length boundary inconsistency

## 1. Summary

RFC 8446 defines the length range for X.509 `cert_data` as `1..2^24-1`. The mbedTLS implementation enforces a narrower range: `128 <= cert_data_len < 64K`. Both the lower bound and the upper bound differ from the standard.

## 2. RFC 8446 Source Text

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2):

```text
case X509:
  opaque cert_data<1..2^24-1>;
```

RFC allows:
- minimum length: `1`
- maximum length: `2^24 - 1`

## 3. Code Description

From `library/ssl_tls13_generic.c`:

```c
/* In theory, the CRT can be up to 2^24 Bytes, but we don't support
 * anything beyond 2^16 = 64K. Otherwise as in the TLS 1.2 code,
 * check that we have a minimum of 128 bytes of data, this is not
 * clear why we need that though.
 */
if ((cert_data_len < 128) || (cert_data_len >= 0x10000)) {
    MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(1, ("bad Certificate message"));
    MBEDTLS_SSL_PEND_FATAL_ALERT(MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_DECODE_ERROR,
                                 MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_DECODE_ERROR);
    return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_DECODE_ERROR;
}
```

mbedTLS enforces:
- lower bound: `cert_data_len >= 128`
- upper bound: `cert_data_len < 0x10000` (i.e., `< 64K`)

## 4. Why This Is Inconsistent

| Bound | RFC 8446 | mbedTLS |
|-------|----------|---------|
| Lower | `1` | `128` |
| Upper | `2^24 - 1` | `< 64K` |

The implementation accepts a significantly narrower range than the standard permits.

## 5. Note on the Code Comment

The comment itself acknowledges the inconsistency:

- Upper bound `< 64K`: the implementation "does not support" beyond `2^16`.
- Lower bound `>= 128`: carried over from TLS 1.2 code, but the comment explicitly states:

```c
this is not clear why we need that though
```

So the lower bound of `128` has no documented justification even within the codebase.

## 6. Conclusion

- mbedTLS enforces stricter length limits on X.509 `cert_data` than RFC 8446 requires.
- The lower bound (`128` vs RFC `1`) has no clear justification even in the code comments.
- The upper bound (`64K` vs RFC `~16M`) is an acknowledged implementation limitation.
items.title: mbedTLS TLS 1.3 X.509 `cert_data` length boundary inconsistency

items.body:
# Empty Client Certificate Uses `no_certificate` Instead of `certificate_required`

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS fails to detect the missing client certificate. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** correctly detect the case where the client sends an empty `Certificate` message after being requested to authenticate.
- But when the server aborts, it sends `no_certificate(41)` instead of the TLS 1.3-mandated `certificate_required(116)`.

Both alert constants are defined in the codebase. The inconsistency is purely in the alert-code selection for this specific TLS 1.3 condition.

Therefore this item is classified as **Not Satisfied** with respect to alert semantics: the failure is detected correctly, but the alert mapping does not match the RFC requirement.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2.1):

```text
   If the client does not send any certificates (i.e., it sends an empty
   Certificate message), the server MAY at its discretion either
   continue the handshake without client authentication or abort the
   handshake with a "certificate_required" alert.
```

### 2.2 Alert constant in the specification

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6.2):

```text
   certificate_required(116)
```

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 Empty peer certificate handling in TLS 1.3 certificate processing

From `library/ssl_tls13_generic.c`:

```c
if (ssl->session_negotiate->peer_cert == NULL) {
    MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(1, ("peer has no certificate"));

#if defined(MBEDTLS_SSL_SRV_C)
    if (ssl->conf->endpoint == MBEDTLS_SSL_IS_SERVER) {
        /* The client was asked for a certificate but didn't send
         * one. The client should know what's going on, so we
         * don't send an alert.
         */
        ssl->session_negotiate->verify_result = MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_MISSING;
        if (authmode == MBEDTLS_SSL_VERIFY_OPTIONAL) {
            return 0;
        } else {
            MBEDTLS_SSL_PEND_FATAL_ALERT(
                MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_NO_CERT,
                MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_NO_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE);
            return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_NO_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE;
        }
    }
#endif /* MBEDTLS_SSL_SRV_C */
}
```

### 3.2 mbedTLS alert definitions

From `include/mbedtls/ssl.h`:

```c
#define MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_NO_CERT               41  /* 0x29 */
...
#define MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_CERT_REQUIRED        116  /* 0x74 */
```

## 4. Inconsistency

The TLS 1.3 requirement says that when:

- the client sends an empty `Certificate` message, and
- the server chooses to abort,

the alert should be `certificate_required`.

However, the mbedTLS TLS 1.3 processing path aborts with:

- `MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_NO_CERT` (`41`)

instead of:

- `MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_CERT_REQUIRED` (`116`)

## 5. Why This Is Inconsistent

The implementation already defines both alert codes, so the issue is not the absence of `certificate_required`.

The inconsistency is that:

- the standard expectation for this specific TLS 1.3 condition is `certificate_required`
- the implementation maps the condition to the older `no_certificate` alert path

In other words, the failure condition is recognized correctly, but the alert-code mapping does not match the TLS 1.3 expectation.

## 6. Impact

- Primary impact: protocol and interoperability semantics mismatch
- Not a direct authentication bypass
- Can affect conformance checking, alert-level interoperability, and precise failure diagnosis

## 7. Conclusion

mbedTLS correctly detects the case where the client sends no certificate after being requested to do so.
But when the server aborts, it uses `no_certificate(41)` rather than `certificate_required(116)`.
items.title: Empty Client Certificate Uses `no_certificate` Instead of `certificate_required`

items.body:
# TLS 1.3 CCS Compatibility Handling Deviates from RFC 8446

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS ignores the CCS compatibility rule entirely. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** implement the basic dummy-CCS format checks: payload length must be 1 and the byte must be `0x01`.
- But the implementation adopts a simplified compatibility strategy that loses two distinctions required by RFC 8446:
  - **CCS time-window (Partially Satisfied):** The RFC allows a dummy CCS to be silently dropped only within the window from the first `ClientHello` to the peer's `Finished`. mbedTLS validates format but does not explicitly enforce this time-window restriction.
  - **Protected CCS rejection (Not Satisfied):** The RFC requires that a *protected* `change_cipher_spec` record `MUST` abort with `unexpected_message`. No dedicated rejection branch for protected CCS was found in the inspected receive path.

Therefore the two items are classified separately: the time-window item as **Partially Satisfied** and the protected-CCS item as **Not Satisfied**.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Appendix D.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#appendix-D.4):

```text
   An implementation may receive an unencrypted record of type
   change_cipher_spec consisting of the single byte value 0x01 at any
   time after the first ClientHello message has been sent or received
   and before the peer's Finished message has been received and MUST
   simply drop it without further processing.  Note that this record may
   appear at a point at the handshake where the implementation is
   expecting protected records, and so it is necessary to detect this
   condition prior to attempting to deprotect the record.  An
   implementation which receives any other change_cipher_spec value or
   which receives a protected change_cipher_spec record MUST abort the
   handshake with an "unexpected_message" alert.  If an implementation
   detects a change_cipher_spec record received before the first
   ClientHello message or after the peer's Finished message, it MUST be
   treated as an unexpected record type
```

### 2.2 Meaning of the requirement

The RFC allows only one narrow compatibility case:

- the CCS record must be unencrypted
- its payload must be exactly one byte `0x01`
- it may only be ignored within the window from the first `ClientHello` to the peer's `Finished`

Outside that case:

- other CCS values must cause abort
- protected CCS records must cause abort
- CCS outside the allowed window must be treated as unexpected

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 TLS 1.3 record preparation treats CCS specially

From `library/ssl_msg.c`:

```c
/*
 * In TLS 1.3, always treat ChangeCipherSpec records
 * as unencrypted. The only thing we do with them is
 * check the length and content and ignore them.
 */
#if defined(MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3)
    if (ssl->transform_in != NULL &&
        ssl->transform_in->tls_version == MBEDTLS_SSL_VERSION_TLS1_3) {
        if (rec->type == MBEDTLS_SSL_MSG_CHANGE_CIPHER_SPEC) {
            done = 1;
        }
    }
#endif /* MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3 */
```

This shows the implementation's high-level strategy in TLS 1.3:

- treat CCS as a compatibility record
- skip normal protected-record handling
- later perform only basic validation and ignore

### 3.2 Actual TLS 1.3 CCS handling path

From `library/ssl_msg.c`:

```c
if (ssl->in_msgtype == MBEDTLS_SSL_MSG_CHANGE_CIPHER_SPEC) {
    if (ssl->in_msglen != 1) {
        return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD;
    }

    if (ssl->in_msg[0] != 1) {
        return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD;
    }

#if defined(MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3)
    if (ssl->tls_version == MBEDTLS_SSL_VERSION_TLS1_3) {
        MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(2,
                              ("Ignore ChangeCipherSpec in TLS 1.3 compatibility mode"));
        return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_CONTINUE_PROCESSING;
    }
#endif /* MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3 */
}
```

This path clearly enforces:

- payload length must be `1`
- payload byte must be `0x01`

But once those checks pass in TLS 1.3, the record is ignored directly.

### 3.3 Decryption can still produce a CCS content type

From `library/ssl_msg.c`:

```c
#if defined(MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3)
    if (transform->tls_version == MBEDTLS_SSL_VERSION_TLS1_3) {
        /* Remove inner padding and infer true content type. */
        ret = ssl_parse_inner_plaintext(data, &rec->data_len,
                                        &rec->type);

        if (ret != 0) {
            return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD;
        }
    }
#endif /* MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3 */
```

```c
/* The record content type may change during decryption,
 * so re-read it. */
ssl->in_msgtype = rec.type;
```

These lines show that after decryption, the effective message type may become `change_cipher_spec`.
In the inspected receive path, no dedicated branch was found that rejects a protected CCS before control reaches the generic TLS 1.3 CCS-ignore logic shown above.

## 4. Inconsistency

### 4.1 CCS time-window: allowed-window handling is weaker than the RFC

The RFC allows a dummy CCS to be ignored only:

- after the first `ClientHello`
- before the peer's `Finished`

However, the inspected mbedTLS TLS 1.3 path:

- validates only length and payload value
- then ignores the CCS in a generic compatibility branch

No equally explicit check was found in this path to enforce the RFC's time-window restriction before ignoring the record.

Therefore, this item is reasonably classified as `Partially Satisfied`:

- the format checks exist
- the strict RFC window semantics are not clearly enforced in the same way

### 4.2 Protected CCS: not explicitly rejected as required

The RFC says that a protected `change_cipher_spec` record:

- `MUST abort the handshake with an "unexpected_message" alert`

But in the inspected mbedTLS receive flow:

- TLS 1.3 CCS is handled through a unified compatibility path
- after decryption, a CCS content type can still reach generic CCS handling
- no dedicated protected-CCS rejection branch was found in the examined logic

Therefore, this item is reasonably classified as `Not Satisfied`.

## 5. Why This Is Inconsistent

The implementation appears to adopt a compatibility-oriented simplification:

- accept only the canonical dummy CCS format
- then ignore it uniformly in TLS 1.3

This is simpler than the RFC rule set, but it loses two protocol distinctions required by the standard:

- whether the CCS appears inside the RFC-allowed handshake window
- whether the CCS record is protected rather than unencrypted

In other words, the implementation captures the common compatibility behavior, but not the full RFC decision logic.

## 6. Impact

- Primary impact: TLS 1.3 conformance mismatch in CCS handling
- Likely interoperability and compliance issue rather than a direct authentication bypass
- Can matter for standards-based testing, edge-case handling, and precise alert behavior

## 7. Conclusion

mbedTLS does implement the basic dummy-CCS format checks required for TLS 1.3 compatibility mode.
However, based on the inspected receive path:

- it does not clearly enforce the RFC's narrow allowed window before ignoring CCS
- it does not show a dedicated abort path for protected CCS records
items.title: TLS 1.3 CCS Compatibility Handling Deviates from RFC 8446

items.body:
# TLS 1.3 KeyShare Ordering Semantics Are Only Partially Covered

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS ignores the `supported_groups` ordering requirement entirely. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** validate that each offered `KeyShareEntry` corresponds to a group in `supported_groups` (via `mbedtls_ssl_named_group_is_offered`).
- But the client is designed to send **only one** `KeyShareEntry`, so the RFC's `MUST appear in the same order` rule — which is conditional on multiple shares being present — is never exercised.
- On the receive side, no explicit ordering check was found that compares the relative order of incoming `client_shares` against `supported_groups`.

Therefore this item is classified as **Partially Satisfied**: the single-share common path does not violate the rule, but the full multi-share ordering semantics are not implemented.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.8](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.8):

```text
   client_shares:  A list of offered KeyShareEntry values in descending
      order of client preference.

   This vector MAY be empty if the client is requesting a
   HelloRetryRequest.  Each KeyShareEntry value MUST correspond to a
   group offered in the "supported_groups" extension and MUST appear in
   the same order.  However, the values MAY be a non-contiguous subset
   of the "supported_groups" extension and MAY omit the most preferred
   groups.
```

The key requirement is the `MUST`:

- if multiple `KeyShareEntry` values are sent
- they must follow the same relative order as the groups listed in `supported_groups`

This does not require the client to send a share for every supported group, but it does require order consistency for whatever subset is sent.

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 Client-side key_share writing path is single-share oriented

From `library/ssl_tls13_client.c`:

```c
static int ssl_tls13_write_key_share_ext(mbedtls_ssl_context *ssl,
                                         unsigned char *buf,
                                         unsigned char *end,
                                         size_t *out_len)
{
    ...
    uint16_t group_id;
    ...
    group_id = ssl->handshake->offered_group_id;
    if (!mbedtls_ssl_tls13_named_group_is_ecdhe(group_id) &&
        !mbedtls_ssl_tls13_named_group_is_ffdh(group_id)) {
        MBEDTLS_SSL_PROC_CHK(ssl_tls13_get_default_group_id(ssl,
                                                            &group_id));
    }
    ...
    /*
     * Dispatch to type-specific key generation function.
     *
     * So far, we're only supporting ECDHE. With the introduction
     * of PQC KEMs, we'll want to have multiple branches, one per
     * type of KEM, and dispatch to the corresponding crypto. And
     * only one key share entry is allowed.
     */
    client_shares = p;
    ...
    MBEDTLS_PUT_UINT16_BE(group_id, group, 0);
    ...
    client_shares_len = p - client_shares;
    ...
    ssl->handshake->offered_group_id = group_id;
    ...
}
```

This path shows that the client:

- selects a single `group_id`
- generates one `KeyShareEntry`
- writes one `key_share` payload based on that one group

The implementation comment is especially important:

- `only one key share entry is allowed`

So the normal sending path does not construct multiple `KeyShareEntry` values.

### 3.2 The same write path is used when composing ClientHello

From `library/ssl_tls13_client.c`:

```c
if (mbedtls_ssl_conf_tls13_is_some_ephemeral_enabled(ssl)) {
    ret = ssl_tls13_write_key_share_ext(ssl, p, end, &ext_len);
    if (ret != 0) {
        return ret;
    }
    p += ext_len;
}
```

This means the outbound `ClientHello` key_share construction goes through the same single-share function.

### 3.3 Server-side parsing iterates over incoming shares, but does not implement an explicit ordering check

From `library/ssl_tls13_server.c`:

```c
static int ssl_tls13_parse_key_shares_ext(mbedtls_ssl_context *ssl,
                                          const unsigned char *buf,
                                          const unsigned char *end)
{
    ...
    while (p < client_shares_end) {
        uint16_t group;
        ...
        if (!mbedtls_ssl_named_group_is_offered(ssl, group) ||
            !mbedtls_ssl_named_group_is_supported(group) ||
            ssl->handshake->offered_group_id != 0) {
            continue;
        }
        ...
        ssl->handshake->offered_group_id = group;
    }
    ...
}
```

This parser can iterate over multiple incoming shares, but in the inspected logic:

- it mainly searches for the first acceptable share
- once one acceptable share has been accepted, subsequent acceptable shares are skipped via `ssl->handshake->offered_group_id != 0`
- no explicit check was found that compares the relative order of `client_shares` against the order in `supported_groups`

## 4. Inconsistency

This item is best understood as a partial semantic gap rather than a proven wrong-order implementation.

What mbedTLS does cover:

- it can send a valid single-share `key_share`
- a single share does not create an ordering conflict by itself
- it can parse multiple incoming shares syntactically

What it does not clearly cover:

- constructing multiple `KeyShareEntry` values in a way that exercises the RFC ordering rule
- explicitly validating that multiple incoming shares appear in the same order as `supported_groups`

So the issue is not "mbedTLS sends shares in the wrong order." Instead, the implementation is centered on a single-share model and does not fully realize the RFC's richer multi-share ordering semantics.

## 5. Why This Is Only Partially Satisfied

The RFC requirement here is a `MUST`, but it is conditional on the presence of multiple `KeyShareEntry` values.

mbedTLS largely avoids this space by design:

- the client normally emits only one share
- the code therefore does not need to make a multi-share ordering choice
- and the receive-side parser does not show a dedicated order-consistency check

That is why `Partially Satisfied` is a reasonable classification:

- there is no obvious contradictory behavior for the single-share case
- but the full multi-share `MUST appear in the same order` semantics are not explicitly implemented

## 6. Conclusion

The standard requires that multiple `KeyShareEntry` values must appear in the same order as `supported_groups`.

mbedTLS does not appear to violate this rule directly in the common path, because its TLS 1.3 client logic is built around sending only one share.
However, that same design means the implementation does not fully cover the RFC's multi-share ordering semantics or show an explicit receive-side ordering check.
items.title: TLS 1.3 KeyShare Ordering Semantics Are Only Partially Covered

items.body:
# TLS 1.3 Cookie Minimum-Length Check Gap

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS lacks a cookie parsing path. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** implement TLS 1.3 cookie parsing: it reads the 16-bit length, checks buffer bounds, and stores the cookie for later echo in the second `ClientHello`.
- But it does **not** explicitly enforce the RFC lower bound `cookie<1..2^16-1>`, meaning a zero-length cookie is accepted and stored without error.
- A targeted probe confirmed this: after injecting a zero-length cookie in a `HelloRetryRequest`, the client stored `cookie_len == 0` and the test passed.

Therefore this item is classified as **Partially Satisfied**: the parsing infrastructure is present, but the RFC-defined minimum-length constraint is not enforced.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Cookie structure and minimum length

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.2):

```text
      struct {
          opaque cookie<1..2^16-1>;
      } Cookie;
```

This means that the TLS 1.3 `cookie` field:

- must have minimum length `1`
- may have maximum length `2^16 - 1`

So a zero-length cookie is outside the specified range.

### 2.2 Client behavior when a cookie is received in HRR

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.2):

```text
   When sending a HelloRetryRequest, the server MAY provide a "cookie"
   extension to the client (this is an exception to the usual rule that
   the only extensions that may be sent are those that appear in the
   ClientHello).  When sending the new ClientHello, the client MUST copy
   the contents of the extension received in the HelloRetryRequest into
   a "cookie" extension in the new ClientHello.  Clients MUST NOT use
   cookies in their initial ClientHello in subsequent connections.
```

If a cookie is received, the client must process a valid TLS 1.3 cookie value. The structure definition above still requires the cookie length to be at least `1`.

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 Client-side cookie parsing does not explicitly reject `cookie_len == 0`

From `library/ssl_tls13_client.c`:

```c
static int ssl_tls13_parse_cookie_ext(mbedtls_ssl_context *ssl,
                                      const unsigned char *buf,
                                      const unsigned char *end)
{
    uint16_t cookie_len;
    const unsigned char *p = buf;
    mbedtls_ssl_handshake_params *handshake = ssl->handshake;

    /* Retrieve length field of cookie */
    MBEDTLS_SSL_CHK_BUF_READ_PTR(p, end, 2);
    cookie_len = MBEDTLS_GET_UINT16_BE(p, 0);
    p += 2;

    MBEDTLS_SSL_CHK_BUF_READ_PTR(p, end, cookie_len);
    MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_BUF(3, "cookie extension", p, cookie_len);

    mbedtls_free(handshake->cookie);
    handshake->cookie_len = 0;
    handshake->cookie = mbedtls_calloc(1, cookie_len);
    if (handshake->cookie == NULL) {
        return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_ALLOC_FAILED;
    }

    memcpy(handshake->cookie, p, cookie_len);
    handshake->cookie_len = cookie_len;

    return 0;
}
```

What this code does:

- reads the 16-bit `cookie_len`
- checks only that the input buffer contains that many bytes
- stores the cookie and its length

What it does not do:

- there is no explicit check that `cookie_len > 0`

So the parser enforces buffer safety, but it does not enforce the RFC lower bound for the cookie length.

### 3.2 Client-side cookie echo is implemented

From `library/ssl_tls13_client.c`:

```c
static int ssl_tls13_write_cookie_ext(mbedtls_ssl_context *ssl,
                                      unsigned char *buf,
                                      unsigned char *end,
                                      size_t *out_len)
{
    ...
    if (handshake->cookie == NULL) {
        MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(3, ("no cookie to send; skip extension"));
        return 0;
    }
    ...
    MBEDTLS_PUT_UINT16_BE(MBEDTLS_TLS_EXT_COOKIE, p, 0);
    MBEDTLS_PUT_UINT16_BE(handshake->cookie_len + 2, p, 2);
    MBEDTLS_PUT_UINT16_BE(handshake->cookie_len, p, 4);
    ...
}
```

Once a zero-length cookie is accepted by the parser, the client-side path is also capable of re-encoding that zero length in the second `ClientHello`.

## 4. Behavioral Test Evidence

### 4.1 Probe description

The probe:

- forces a TLS 1.3 `HelloRetryRequest`
- injects a modified HRR carrying a zero-length cookie
- verifies whether the client accepts it

### 4.2 Assertion showing zero-length cookie acceptance

From `tests/suites/test_suite_ssl.function`:

```c
TEST_EQUAL(client_ep.ssl.handshake->cookie_len, 0);
```

This confirms that after the modified HRR is processed, the client stores `cookie_len == 0`.

### 4.3 Test execution result

```text
TLS 1.3 - HRR cookie probe (default absent + injected zero-length cookie)
tls13_hrr_cookie_zero_length_probe
PASS (1 / 1 tests)
```

The intended probe behavior was reproduced successfully, including acceptance of the injected zero-length cookie.

## 5. Inconsistency

This item is reasonably classified as `Partially Satisfied`.

Reason:

- mbedTLS has a working TLS 1.3 cookie parsing path
- but it does not explicitly enforce the RFC lower bound `cookie<1..>`
- the added probe showed that a zero-length cookie can be accepted and stored

So this is not a missing feature. It is a concrete range-validation gap relative to the TLS 1.3 structure definition.

## 6. Why This Is Inconsistent

The parser currently prioritizes:

- reading the encoded length
- ensuring the buffer is large enough
- storing the value for later use

But the RFC requires more than buffer safety here. It also defines a semantic validity range for `cookie`, and that range excludes zero length.

In other words:

- the implementation checks whether the field can be read safely
- it does not fully check whether the field value is valid under the RFC

## 7. Conclusion

The evidence supports a real standards-relevant boundary-check issue:

- TLS 1.3 defines `cookie` as `opaque cookie<1..2^16-1>`
- mbedTLS does not explicitly reject `cookie_len == 0`
- the added probe demonstrated that a zero-length cookie can be accepted and stored
items.title: TLS 1.3 Cookie Minimum-Length Check Gap

# Empty Server Certificate Chain Uses `no_certificate` Instead of `decode_error`

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS fails to detect an empty server certificate chain. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** correctly detect the case where the server sends an empty `certificate_list` and aborts the handshake.
- But the alert sent is `no_certificate(41)` instead of the TLS 1.3-mandated `decode_error(50)`.

Both alert constants are defined in the codebase. The inconsistency is purely in the alert-code selection: RFC 8446 classifies an empty server `Certificate` message as a decoding error, not a certificate-absence condition.

Therefore this item is classified as **Not Satisfied** with respect to alert semantics: the failure is detected correctly, but the alert mapping does not match the RFC requirement.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Server certificate list must not be empty

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2):

```text
   The server's certificate_list MUST always be non-empty.  A client
   will send an empty certificate_list if it does not have an
   appropriate certificate to send in response to the server's
   authentication request.
```

This establishes the basic TLS 1.3 rule:

- a server is not allowed to send an empty certificate chain

### 2.2 Required alert if the server sends an empty Certificate message

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2.1):

```text
   If the server supplies an empty Certificate message, the client MUST
   abort the handshake with a "decode_error" alert.
```

The standard does not merely require the client to fail. It also specifies the alert to use:

- `decode_error`

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 Empty certificate_list is parsed as `peer_cert == NULL`

From `library/ssl_tls13_generic.c`:

```c
/* This is used by ssl_tls13_validate_certificate() */
if (certificate_list_len == 0) {
    ssl->session_negotiate->peer_cert = NULL;
    ret = 0;
    goto exit;
}
```

When the received TLS 1.3 `Certificate` message contains an empty `certificate_list`, mbedTLS records the peer certificate as absent.

### 3.2 Client-side validation path uses `MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_NO_CERT`

From `library/ssl_tls13_generic.c`:

```c
if (ssl->session_negotiate->peer_cert == NULL) {
    MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(1, ("peer has no certificate"));

#if defined(MBEDTLS_SSL_CLI_C)
    /* Regardless of authmode, the server is not allowed to send an empty
     * certificate chain. (Last paragraph before 4.4.2.1 in RFC 8446: "The
     * server's certificate_list MUST always be non-empty.") With authmode
     * optional/none, we continue the handshake if we can't validate the
     * server's cert, but we still break it if no certificate was sent. */
    if (ssl->conf->endpoint == MBEDTLS_SSL_IS_CLIENT) {
        MBEDTLS_SSL_PEND_FATAL_ALERT(MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_NO_CERT,
                                     MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_FATAL_ALERT_MESSAGE);
        return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_FATAL_ALERT_MESSAGE;
    }
#endif /* MBEDTLS_SSL_CLI_C */
}
```

This branch correctly recognizes the problem — the server sent no certificate and the client must abort — but the alert selected is `MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_NO_CERT` instead of `MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_DECODE_ERROR`.

### 3.3 Alert constant definitions

From `include/mbedtls/ssl.h`:

```c
#define MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_NO_CERT               41  /* 0x29 */
...
#define MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_DECODE_ERROR          50  /* 0x32 */
```

Both alert values exist in the implementation, but the empty-server-certificate path chooses `no_certificate(41)` rather than `decode_error(50)`.

## 4. Inconsistency

The TLS 1.3 rule for this case is explicit:

- if the server sends an empty `Certificate` message
- the client `MUST` abort
- and the alert must be `decode_error`

mbedTLS does abort, but it maps the condition to `no_certificate(41)` instead of `decode_error(50)`.

So the issue is not failure detection. The issue is alert-code selection.

## 5. Why This Is Inconsistent

The implementation treats the situation as "the peer has no certificate." That interpretation is operationally understandable, but it is not the alert mapping required by TLS 1.3 for this specific server-side protocol violation.

RFC 8446 classifies the case as a malformed or invalid `Certificate` message from the server and therefore requires `decode_error`.

In other words:

- mbedTLS recognizes the invalid condition
- but uses a legacy-style certificate absence alert instead of the TLS 1.3 mandated decoding alert

## 6. Impact

- Primary impact: TLS 1.3 conformance mismatch in alert behavior
- Not a direct authentication bypass
- Can affect protocol conformance checks, interoperability diagnostics, and standards-based negative testing

## 7. Conclusion

The evidence supports a real alert mapping inconsistency:

- TLS 1.3 requires `decode_error` when the server supplies an empty `Certificate` message
- mbedTLS instead aborts with `no_certificate`
items.title: Empty Server Certificate Chain Uses `no_certificate` Instead of `decode_error`

# Zero-Length TLSInnerPlaintext for Handshake or Alert Falls into `INVALID_RECORD`

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS accepts zero-length inner content for Handshake or Alert records. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** reject these malformed records — the connection does not continue normally.
- But the rejection path returns `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD` rather than terminating with an `unexpected_message` alert as RFC 8446 explicitly requires.

Therefore, this item is a standards-relevant alert-semantics mismatch: the invalid input is rejected, but the error/alert semantics do not align with the RFC-specified `unexpected_message` requirement.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 5.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-5.4):

```text
   Application Data records may contain a zero-length
   TLSInnerPlaintext.content if the sender desires.  This permits
   generation of plausibly sized cover traffic in contexts where the
   presence or absence of activity may be sensitive.  Implementations
   MUST NOT send Handshake and Alert records that have a zero-length
   TLSInnerPlaintext.content; if such a message is received, the
   receiving implementation MUST terminate the connection with an
   "unexpected_message" alert.
```

This rule is specific:

- zero-length `application_data` is allowed
- zero-length `handshake` and `alert` inner content is not allowed
- if such a record is received, the required alert is `unexpected_message`

### 2.2 Related TLSInnerPlaintext scanning rule

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 5.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-5.4):

```text
   Implementations MUST limit their scanning to the cleartext returned
   from the AEAD decryption.  If a receiving implementation does not
   find a non-zero octet in the cleartext, it MUST terminate the
   connection with an "unexpected_message" alert.
```

So if the implementation cannot recover a non-zero inner content type, the RFC again requires `unexpected_message`.

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 TLS 1.3 inner plaintext parsing returns `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD`

From `library/ssl_msg.c`:

```c
#if defined(MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3)
    if (transform->tls_version == MBEDTLS_SSL_VERSION_TLS1_3) {
        /* Remove inner padding and infer true content type. */
        ret = ssl_parse_inner_plaintext(data, &rec->data_len,
                                        &rec->type);

        if (ret != 0) {
            return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD;
        }
    }
#endif /* MBEDTLS_SSL_PROTO_TLS1_3 */
```

When the TLS 1.3 inner plaintext scan fails, the receive path returns `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD`. There is no explicit `MBEDTLS_SSL_PEND_FATAL_ALERT(... UNEXPECTED_MESSAGE ...)` in this path.

### 3.2 Handshake records with too-short content also fall into `INVALID_RECORD`

From `library/ssl_msg.c`:

```c
if (ssl->in_hsfraglen == 0) {
    ...
    if (ssl->in_msglen < mbedtls_ssl_hs_hdr_len(ssl)) {
        MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(1, ("handshake message too short: %" MBEDTLS_PRINTF_SIZET,
                                  ssl->in_msglen));
        return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD;
    }
    ...
}
```

Malformed TLS 1.3 handshake content is primarily classified as `INVALID_RECORD`.

### 3.3 Alert messages with invalid content length also fall into `INVALID_RECORD`

From `library/ssl_msg.c`:

```c
if (ssl->in_msgtype == MBEDTLS_SSL_MSG_ALERT) {
    if (ssl->in_msglen != 2) {
        MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(1, ("invalid alert message, len: %" MBEDTLS_PRINTF_SIZET,
                                  ssl->in_msglen));
        return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD;
    }
    ...
}
```

Again, the receive path rejects the input through `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD` rather than an explicit `unexpected_message` alert mapping.

### 3.4 `INVALID_RECORD` is distinct from `UNEXPECTED_MESSAGE`

From `include/mbedtls/ssl.h`:

```c
#define MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD                    -0x7200
#define MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_UNEXPECTED_MESSAGE                -0x7700
...
#define MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_UNEXPECTED_MESSAGE    10  /* 0x0A */
```

`MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD` is not the same internal error as `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_UNEXPECTED_MESSAGE`, and it is not automatically equivalent to the RFC alert `unexpected_message`.

## 4. Inconsistency

The TLS 1.3 RFC requires that:

- if a Handshake or Alert record is received with zero-length `TLSInnerPlaintext.content`
- or if no non-zero inner content-type octet can be found
- the connection must be terminated with an `unexpected_message` alert

In the inspected mbedTLS receive path:

- the failure is rejected
- but the rejection primarily appears as `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD`
- no equally explicit `unexpected_message` alert mapping was found in this path

So the implementation does reject the invalid input, but the error/alert semantics do not line up cleanly with the RFC requirement.

## 5. Why This Is Only Partially Satisfied

This is not a complete functional miss:

- the invalid record is not accepted
- the connection does not continue normally

However, the RFC does not only require rejection. It also requires a particular alert semantic: `unexpected_message`.

mbedTLS instead uses a more generic invalid-record classification in the relevant receive path.

That is why `Partially Satisfied` is the right characterization:

- behaviorally, the record is rejected
- semantically, the rejection path is not aligned with the RFC-specified alert

## 6. Conclusion

The relevant TLS 1.3 rule is the `TLSInnerPlaintext` rule for zero-length Handshake and Alert content.

The RFC requires `unexpected_message`. The inspected mbedTLS path instead primarily returns `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_RECORD`.
items.title: Zero-Length TLSInnerPlaintext for Handshake or Alert Falls into `INVALID_RECORD`
