[Originally reported privately by @LiD0209; after analysis we do not think this particular issue leads to a vulnerability, so we are tracking it publicly.]

# Some TLS 1.3 `extension_data` Parsers Accept Trailing Bytes Instead of Failing

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS fails to check extension boundaries at all. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** enforce outer extension-block boundaries in several places.
- But some inner TLS 1.3 `extension_data` parsers do **not** verify that the extension payload is consumed exactly.
- As a result, a malformed extension can be accepted when its valid prefix is followed by extra trailing bytes.

So the actual inconsistency is narrower and more precise than a generic "extension block integrity" failure:

- the outer extension block may be length-valid
- but some individual extension parsers still accept trailing data that should make the extension invalid

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Outer extensions block must be exact

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.1.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.2):

```text
   If negotiating a version of TLS prior to 1.3,
   a server MUST check that the message either contains no data after
   legacy_compression_methods or that it contains a valid extensions
   block with no data following.  If not, then it MUST abort the
   handshake with a "decode_error" alert.
```

This wording is about the outer extensions block, but it shows the [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.1.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.2) style clearly:

- length-delimited structures are expected to be exact
- trailing bytes are not meant to be silently tolerated

### 2.2 Messages that do not match the syntax must fail with `decode_error`

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 6](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6):

```text
   Peers which receive a message which
   cannot be parsed according to the syntax (e.g., have a length
   extending beyond the message boundary or contain an out-of-range
   length) MUST terminate the connection with a "decode_error" alert.
```

And from [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6.2):

```text
   decode_error:  A message could not be decoded because some field was
      out of the specified range or the length of the message was
      incorrect.  This alert is used for errors where the message does
      not conform to the formal protocol syntax.
```

These rules do not say in one sentence that "`extension_data` must be fully consumed", but that requirement is implied when:

- `extension_data` is defined as one exact structure
- extra bytes remain after that structure
- the received bytes therefore no longer match the formal syntax of that extension

### 2.3 Several TLS 1.3 extensions are defined as exact structures

For example, the Cookie extension in [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.2) is:

```text
      struct {
          opaque cookie<1..2^16-1>;
      } Cookie;
```

The PSK key exchange modes extension in [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.9](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.9) is:

```text
      struct {
          PskKeyExchangeMode ke_modes<1..255>;
      } PskKeyExchangeModes;
```

And the `post_handshake_auth` extension in [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.6](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.6) is even more explicit:

```text
      struct {} PostHandshakeAuth;

   The "extension_data" field of the "post_handshake_auth" extension is
   zero length.
```

In all of these cases, the extension payload is not a free-form prefix. It is the full encoding of the specified structure.

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 `psk_key_exchange_modes` parser does not verify full consumption

In [ssl_tls13_server.c](../mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls13_server.c:108), `ssl_tls13_parse_key_exchange_modes_ext()`:

```c
static int ssl_tls13_parse_key_exchange_modes_ext(mbedtls_ssl_context *ssl,
                                                  const unsigned char *buf,
                                                  const unsigned char *end)
{
    const unsigned char *p = buf;
    size_t ke_modes_len;
    int ke_modes = 0;

    MBEDTLS_SSL_CHK_BUF_READ_PTR(p, end, 1);
    ke_modes_len = *p++;
    ...
    MBEDTLS_SSL_CHK_BUF_READ_PTR(p, end, ke_modes_len);

    while (ke_modes_len-- != 0) {
        switch (*p++) {
            ...
        }
    }

    ssl->handshake->tls13_kex_modes = ke_modes;
    return 0;
}
```

After reading exactly `ke_modes_len` bytes, the function returns success. It does **not** check that `p == end`.

So if the payload is:

- valid `PskKeyExchangeModes`
- followed by one extra byte

the parser still returns success.

### 3.2 `cookie` parser does not verify full consumption

In [ssl_tls13_client.c](../mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls13_client.c:529), `ssl_tls13_parse_cookie_ext()`:

```c
static int ssl_tls13_parse_cookie_ext(mbedtls_ssl_context *ssl,
                                      const unsigned char *buf,
                                      const unsigned char *end)
{
    uint16_t cookie_len;
    const unsigned char *p = buf;
    ...
    MBEDTLS_SSL_CHK_BUF_READ_PTR(p, end, 2);
    cookie_len = MBEDTLS_GET_UINT16_BE(p, 0);
    p += 2;

    MBEDTLS_SSL_CHK_BUF_READ_PTR(p, end, cookie_len);
    ...
    memcpy(handshake->cookie, p, cookie_len);
    handshake->cookie_len = cookie_len;

    return 0;
}
```

Again, the parser checks that the declared cookie bytes are present, but it does **not** verify that no bytes remain after them.

### 3.3 The outer parser skips the whole extension after inner success

This matters because the outer extension loop treats the entire `extension_data` as consumed once the inner parser returns success.

That means trailing bytes are not:

- parsed as a separate field
- rejected as invalid

They are effectively ignored as leftover bytes inside an accepted extension.

## 4. Focused Reproduction

To avoid transcript side effects from full-handshake mutation, a focused parser harness was used from the local output artifacts.

This harness directly exercised parser logic equivalent to:

- [ssl_tls13_server.c:108](../mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls13_server.c:108) `ssl_tls13_parse_key_exchange_modes_ext`
- [ssl_tls13_client.c:529](../mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls13_client.c:529) `ssl_tls13_parse_cookie_ext`
- [ssl_tls13_client.c:126](../mbedtls-development/library/ssl_tls13_client.c:126) `ssl_tls13_parse_alpn_ext`

The observed results were:

```text
psk_exact: ret=0 accepted=1
psk_trailing: ret=0 accepted=1
cookie_exact: ret=0 accepted=1
cookie_trailing: ret=0 accepted=1
alpn_exact: ret=0 accepted=1
alpn_trailing: ret=0 accepted=1
```

These results show that, for the tested parsers:

- a syntactically exact payload is accepted
- a payload with trailing bytes is also accepted

So the issue is reproducible.

## 5. Inconsistency

[RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2) models `extension_data` as the encoding of a specific structure, not as:

- a valid prefix
- plus arbitrary ignored suffix bytes

But the tested mbedTLS parsers behave as follows:

- they validate only the prefix they care about
- they return success without proving that the extension payload is exhausted
- the remaining bytes are then skipped by the outer extension walker

That is inconsistent with the syntax-oriented model in [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2) and with the `decode_error` rules for malformed message structure in [Section 6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6.2).

## 6. Why This Matters

This is primarily a parser-leniency problem:

- malformed messages may be accepted instead of rejected
- standards-based negative tests can produce false passes
- different TLS implementations may disagree on whether the same message is valid

This does **not** immediately imply a high-severity security break by itself, but it is still a real conformance and robustness issue.

## 7. Conclusion

The evidence supports a real issue, but it is best described precisely:

- mbedTLS does perform outer extension boundary checks
- however, some inner TLS 1.3 `extension_data` parsers do not enforce full consumption
- therefore, trailing bytes can be silently accepted inside otherwise valid extensions

So a clearer characterization than the original generic wording is:

`Some TLS 1.3 extension_data parsers lack exact-consumption checks, allowing trailing bytes to be accepted instead of rejected as malformed.`
items.title: TLS 1.3: Accept Trailing Bytes Instead of Failing

items.body:
### Summary

The bitmask used in `mbedtls_x509_crt_profile.allowed_pks` is designed for `mbedtls_pk_type_t`, at least that is how it is used during client cert validation:

```c
// mbedtls-4.1.0/library/x509_crt.c:3062
    /* Check the type and size of the key */
    pk_type = mbedtls_pk_get_type(&crt->pk);  // this returns MBEDTLS_PK_ECKEY in my case

    if (x509_profile_check_pk_alg(profile, (mbedtls_pk_sigalg_t) pk_type) != 0) {

// x509_profile_check_pk_alg checks: profile->allowed_pks & MBEDTLS_X509_ID_FLAG(pk_alg)
```

`mbedtls_pk_type_t` has become private, and the public replacement, `mbedtls_pk_sigalg_t` is missing a few entries, notably `MBEDTLS_PK_ECKEY`, which I see in my client certificates (bp256).

### System information

Mbed TLS version (number or commit id): 4.1.0
Operating system and version: any
Configuration (if not default, please attach `mbedtls_config.h`):
Compiler and options (if you used a pre-built binary, please indicate how you obtained it):
Additional environment information:

### Expected behavior

The certificate profile can be configured to accept just ECC certs without workarounds.

### Actual behavior

My ECC cert is rejected with `MBEDTLS_X509_BADCERT_BAD_PK` (when only using public defines).

I have to use `MBEDTLS_PK_ECKEY` (declared as private) or manually specify the bits to make the server accept the cert (e.g.
`MBEDTLS_X509_ID_FLAG(MBEDTLS_PK_SIGALG_ECDSA + 1)`).

### Steps to reproduce

* Attempt to limit certificate profile on server to support only ECC keys
* Client connects with ECC key

items.title: Setting `mbedtls_x509_crt_profile.allowed_pks` requires use of private `mbedtls_pk_type_t` value

items.body:
`mbedtls_x509_crt_info()` fails on some certificates with the error -10624 = `MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL` = "-0x2980 - X509 - Destination buffer is too small", even when given a very large output buffer. Inspecting the behavior, I can see that the problem is in `mbedtls_x509_dn_gets()`. It has an internal buffer `char s[MBEDTLS_X509_MAX_DN_NAME_SIZE]` where it writes a printable representation of each name. If the printable representation doesn't fit, it returns `MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`. There are several problems here:

* `XXX_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL` means “if you try again with a larger output buffer, it will either work or encounter a different problem”. It's a bug to return this error if a larger output buffer won't help.
* `MBEDTLS_X509_MAX_DN_NAME_SIZE` seems to be an arbitrary limit. It's documented as “Maximum value size of a DN entry”, but I don't know where this comes from.
* `MBEDTLS_X509_MAX_DN_NAME_SIZE` seems to be intended to be the maximum length of a DN entry, but here it's applied to a printable encoding which can expand each byte to up to 3 characters, plus a little overhead for metacharacters. So I think the practical limit is actually around 80 characters.
* I don't understand why this function uses an intermediate stack buffer and reinvents byte-to-hex printing rather than writing directly to the output buffer. It uses `snprintf` calls which already check that there is enough room and would handle hex printing.

Observed with Mbed TLS 3.6.6.

Here are two certificates that trigger this bug, both from CCADB.

items.body:
### Summary
In PSA-client-only builds (MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C disabled, e.g. NS-side mbedtls dispatching to a secure PSA service like TF-M), TLS 1.2 ECDHE handshakes fail because the premaster-secret union is sized below PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE.

The local ECP_LIGHT cascade does not run in this configuration, so MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES collapses to 1. The `_pms_ecdh` and `_pms_ecdhe_psk` members of `union mbedtls_ssl_premaster_secret` are declared in terms of that macro, so the whole union ends up ~1 byte in builds that have only ECDHE-RSA / ECDHE-ECDSA enabled (no PSK, no ECJPAKE).

ssl_tls12_client.c:2436 passes sizeof(premaster) to psa_raw_key_agreement(); it returns PSA_ERROR_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL, mbedtls maps that to MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_HW_ACCEL_FAILED and aborts the handshake. No memory corruption, just a deterministic handshake failure that any PSA-client deployment hits.

### System information

Mbed TLS version (number or commit id): 6a08a7eb7 (development HEAD) Operating system and version: Zephyr RTOS, any Configuration (if not default, please attach `mbedtls_config.h`): 
- MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C disabled 
- MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_CLIENT enabled (PSA dispatched to TF-M)
- MBEDTLS_KEY_EXCHANGE_ECDHE_RSA_ENABLED and/or MBEDTLS_KEY_EXCHANGE_ECDHE_ECDSA_ENABLED 
- PSK / ECDHE-PSK / ECJPAKE key exchanges disabled
- PSA_WANT_ALG_ECDH + at least one PSA_WANT_ECC_* curve Compiler and options (if you used a pre-built binary, please indicate how you obtained it): arm-zephyr-eabi-gcc, default Zephyr build for an STM32U585-class target (Cortex-M33 with TF-M secure side)
Additional environment information: Reproduces with both TF-M-backed PSA and any other external PSA service that disables MBEDTLS_PSA_CRYPTO_C in the NS-side build.

### Expected behavior

TLS 1.2 ECDHE-RSA or ECDHE-ECDSA handshake completes successfully in a PSA-client-only build.

### Actual behavior

Handshake aborts at psa_raw_key_agreement() with PSA_ERROR_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL, surfaced as MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_HW_ACCEL_FAILED. The premaster union is sized to ~1 byte because every member is gated on a feature macro that is either disabled (PSK family, ECJPAKE) or expands to a 1-byte declaration (`_pms_ecdh[MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES]` with MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES == 1).

### Steps to reproduce

1. Build mbedtls with the configuration above (PSA client only, ECDHE key exchanges enabled, PSK / ECDHE-PSK / ECJPAKE disabled). 
2. Start a TLS 1.2 ECDHE-RSA or ECDHE-ECDSA handshake against any peer that accepts it.
3. Observe handshake failure at the key-exchange step with MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_HW_ACCEL_FAILED.

### Additional information

Proposed fix: introduce MBEDTLS_SSL_PMS_ECDH_SIZE defined as max(PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE, MBEDTLS_ECP_MAX_BYTES) and use it for `_pms_ecdh` and `_pms_ecdhe_psk`. PSA_RAW_KEY_AGREEMENT_OUTPUT_MAX_SIZE is always defined and sized by the enabled PSA_WANT_ECC_* curves, so legacy ECP builds keep their existing buffer size and PSA-client builds get a sufficient one. Happy to follow up with a PR.

`mbedtls_ssl_conf_get_user_data_p()`, `mbedtls_ssl_get_user_data_p()`, `mbedtls_ssl_conf_get_user_data_n()`, `mbedtls_ssl_get_user_data_n()` should take a `const mbedtls_ssl_xxx*` argument, not `mbedtls_ssl_xxx*`. Otherwise they can't be used cleanly in callbacks that take a const pointer to the config/context.

I don't think that affects user callbacks since they always take a non-const pointer. I ran into this while writing test code.

items.body:
Found an issue where mbedTLS silently drops all X.509 extensions when the `[3] EXPLICIT` wrapper tag is changed to `[1]` or `[2]`. Tested on mbedTLS 2.28.

RFC 5280 §4.1.2.9 says the extensions field must use tag `0xa3` (`[3] EXPLICIT`). If you take a valid cert and change just that one byte to `0xa1` (issuerUniqueID tag) or `0xa2` (subjectUniqueID tag), mbedTLS accepts the cert but produces a `mbedtls_x509_crt` with:
- `ext_types == 0`
- `ca_istrue == 0` 
- `key_usage == 0`
- `max_pathlen == 0`

All critical constraints just disappear. The cert DER bytes are still there but the parsed structure reports no extensions.

**The bug:** In `library/x509_crt.c::x509_get_crt_ext()`, when the tag isn't `[3]`, the function treats it as "extensions absent" and returns success:

```c
if( ( ret = mbedtls_asn1_get_tag( p, end, &len,
        MBEDTLS_ASN1_CONTEXT_SPECIFIC | MBEDTLS_ASN1_CONSTRUCTED | 3 ) ) != 0 )
{
    if( ret == MBEDTLS_ERR_ASN1_UNEXPECTED_TAG )
        return( 0 );   /* ← silently skips on wrong wrapper tag */
    ...
}
```

The OPTIONAL-skip on UNEXPECTED_TAG is correct when extensions are genuinely absent, but wrong when a *different* context-specific tag is present — that's a malformed cert, not an optional field.

**12 other implementations reject it:**
OpenSSL 3.x, OpenSSL 4.x, LibreSSL, BoringSSL, GnuTLS, wolfSSL, Botan, NSS, ANSSI x509-parser, rusticata x509-parser, rustls-webpki, briansmith webpki — all reject with parse errors. Only mbedTLS and BearSSL accept.

**Reproduction:**
```bash
# Generate a cert with critical extensions
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout /tmp/k.pem -out /tmp/c.pem \
    -days 365 -nodes -subj "/CN=Test" -sha256 \
    -addext "keyUsage=critical,digitalSignature" \
    -addext "basicConstraints=critical,CA:FALSE"
openssl x509 -in /tmp/c.pem -outform DER -out /tmp/base.der

# Mutate the extensions wrapper tag 0xa3 → 0xa2
python3 -c "
data = bytearray(open('/tmp/base.der','rb').read())
for i in range(len(data) - 2):
    if data[i] == 0xa3 and data[i+2] == 0x30:
        data[i] = 0xa2
        break
open('/tmp/poc.der','wb').write(data)
"

# mbedTLS accepts (ext_types=0), everyone else rejects
```

**Fix:** Only accept the optional-skip when there's genuinely no more data:

```diff
- if( ret == MBEDTLS_ERR_ASN1_UNEXPECTED_TAG )
-     return( 0 );
+ if( ret == MBEDTLS_ERR_ASN1_UNEXPECTED_TAG && *p == end )
+     return( 0 );    /* genuinely absent */
+ if( ret == MBEDTLS_ERR_ASN1_UNEXPECTED_TAG )
+     return( MBEDTLS_ERROR_ADD( MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_INVALID_EXTENSIONS, ret ) );
```

Found through 14-implementation differential fuzzing (PathDiff diff_fuzz_v2). BearSSL has the same bug; I'll file separately with them.

items.body:
### Suggested enhancement

Currently, benchmark.c provides benchmarks for the legacy mbedtls APIs. As the default crypto API is becoming PSA based, also the PSA APIs shall be benchmarked and the results compared to the legacy mbedtls API's performance.

Additionally, there is currently no benchmark for actual end-to-end TLS throughput. It shall be benchmarked as well, not over the wire but simply using buffers to write to and read from.

In my benchmark experiments, I came to the following conclusions:

1. AES CBC ciphersuites without ETM are damn slow due to the constant time HMAC used to counter Lucky13. However, they are so slow that especially embedded systems might be stuck in doing almost nothing else than calculating HMACs. Hence, it would be good to enforce ETM on AES CBC ciphersuites as only countermeasure for Lucky13 and not support non-ETM CBC ciphersuites anymore.
2. NULL ciphersuites also use constant time HMACs, which makes them almost as slow as the AES CBC ciphersuites without ETM. However, I do not see why NULL ciphersuites would need constant time HMACs. Which attack is supposed to be countered by this countermeasure? If there is no valid reason to use constant time HMACs, use regular HMACs instead.
3. Compared to the legacy mbedtls APIs, PSA APIs are highly inefficient. On every crypto operation, key management operations are performed, for example, to lock the key slot and get the key, verify that a certain operation is allowed to be done with a key, ... Especially for short TLS records, the overhead is significant and reduces the TLS throughput. If a key in a certain keyslot is chosen to be used as TLS session key, it should be sufficient to perform all those key management checks once, when the session is setup, but during frame processing, the implementation shall stupidly just perform the crypto operations with no further overhead.
4. Using PSA APIs to perform AES GCM operations during SSL encryption and decryption, the `psa_aead_encrypt` function is called in `mbedtls_ssl_encrypt_buf`, for example. Compared to the legacy API, `psa_aead_encrypt` internally calls `psa_aead_setup` on every single frame. Which calls `mbedtls_gcm_setkey` that calculates `H` for GCM operations from the AES key. With the legacy mbedtls API, `mbedtls_gcm_setkey` was only called once per session and not once per frame. Hence, the use of PSA APIs significantly reduces the TLS throughput of AES GCM. Here, it would be better to cache `H` with the AES keys that are used for GCM operations, so that it does not need to be recalculated everytime.


### Justification

Mbed TLS needs this because otherwise we cannot measure the significant performance decrease that some architectural design decisions introduce. Next step would be to create additional tickets to solve the inefficiencies.
items.title: Benchmark PSA API and TLS throughput
